foundation_variants.name: Idempotent by Default
foundation_variants.description: Every Oban worker is idempotent. Assume retries will happen. Track completion via a database flag or ledger. Never design a worker that cannot be safely re-run.
foundation_variants.skill_md:
---
name: worker-philosophy--idempotent-by-default
description: Frame every Oban worker as idempotent-by-default. Assume retries, use completion flags, prefer Ecto.Multi for transactional enqueue.
---

# Worker Philosophy — Idempotent by Default

## Quick Start

Before writing an Oban worker, ask: 'what happens if this runs twice?' Oban WILL retry on transient failures — design for that as the default.

## When to use

- Every time you write a new `use Oban.Worker` module.
- When reviewing a worker for production readiness.
- When a worker touches external systems (payments, emails, webhooks).

## Workflow

1. Identify the external side effect (email sent, charge made, webhook delivered).
2. Add a completion flag on the domain record (`sent_at`, `charged_at`, `processed_at`).
3. In `perform/1`, check the flag BEFORE running the side effect. If set, return `:ok`.
4. Wrap the side effect + flag update in an `Ecto.Multi` to make them atomic.
5. When inserting the job itself, use `Ecto.Multi` with `Oban.insert/3` so data + job enqueue commit together.

See `${CLAUDE_SKILL_DIR}/references/guide.md` for worked examples.

## Examples

- `MyApp.Workers.PaymentWorker` checks `charge.processed_at` before calling Stripe.
- `MyApp.Accounts.register_user/1` uses `Ecto.Multi` to insert a User and enqueue a welcome email atomically.

## Common mistakes

- Writing perform/1 without a completion check.
- Two-call insert: `Repo.insert(user); MyWorker.new(...) |> Oban.insert()` (not atomic).
- Setting `max_attempts: 1` to avoid idempotency — that abandons Oban's retry semantics.

capability_variants.name: Standard perform/1
capability_variants.description: Destructure %Oban.Job{args: %{"key" => val}}. String keys only. Return :ok, {:error, reason}, {:cancel, reason}, or {:snooze, seconds}.
capability_variants.skill_md:
---
name: perform-callback-basics--standard
description: The canonical perform/1 shape. Destructure the Oban.Job struct. Pattern-match on string keys. Return from the documented protocol.
---

# perform/1 Basics — Standard

## Quick Start

Every Oban worker's perform/1 must: (1) destructure %Oban.Job{}, (2) match args on string keys, (3) return one of :ok / {:ok, _} / {:error, _} / {:cancel, _} / {:snooze, _}.

## When to use

- Every new worker.
- When reviewing a worker that pattern-matches with atom keys.

## Workflow

1. Write `def perform(%Oban.Job{args: %{"key" => value}}) do`.
2. Run the business logic.
3. Return from the documented protocol.

See `${CLAUDE_SKILL_DIR}/references/guide.md` for return-value details.

## Examples

- `def perform(%Oban.Job{args: %{"user_id" => id}}), do: MyApp.Accounts.touch(id)`
- `def perform(%Oban.Job{attempt: a, args: %{"id" => id}})` when you need attempt number.

## Common mistakes

- `def perform(%{user_id: id})` — atom keys fail at runtime (args are JSON).
- Returning `{:noreply, _}` from GenServer muscle memory.

capability_variants.name: String Keys, No Structs
capability_variants.description: Args are JSON. Use string keys. Never pass structs. Convert Decimal/DateTime/NaiveDateTime to ISO strings before enqueue, parse on the way out.
capability_variants.skill_md:
---
name: args-serialization--strings-only
description: Oban args are JSON-encoded. Use string keys everywhere. Never pass structs. Stringify Decimal/DateTime for round-trip.
---

# Args Serialization — Strings Only

## Quick Start

Oban serializes args as JSON. Only JSON-native types round-trip: strings, numbers, booleans, nil, lists, maps with string keys. Everything else must be converted.

## When to use

- Every enqueue site.
- Every perform/1 destructure.

## Workflow

1. Build args with string keys: `%{"user_id" => user.id}`.
2. Convert non-JSON types: `DateTime.to_iso8601/1`, `Decimal.to_string/1`.
3. In perform/1, parse back: `DateTime.from_iso8601/1`, `Decimal.new/1`.

See `${CLAUDE_SKILL_DIR}/references/guide.md` for the full list of unsafe types.

## Examples

- Decimal: `enqueue(id, %Decimal{} = amount) -> %{"amount" => Decimal.to_string(amount)}`
- DateTime: `%{"at" => DateTime.to_iso8601(dt)}`

## Common mistakes

- `%{user_id: user.id}` — atom keys fail at runtime.
- `%{user: %User{}}` — structs lose __struct__ field in JSON.
- Passing tuples (JSON arrays lose arity).

capability_variants.name: Trust Oban's Retries
capability_variants.description: Let Oban handle retries. Return {:error, reason} for transient failures. Override backoff/1 when you need custom behavior — never Process.sleep.
capability_variants.skill_md:
---
name: retry-strategy--trust-oban
description: Return {:error, reason} for transient failures and trust Oban's exponential backoff. Override backoff/1 for custom delays — never hand-roll with Process.sleep.
---

# Retry Strategy — Trust Oban

## Quick Start

Oban has retries built in. Don't reinvent them.

## When to use

- Any worker with transient failure modes (network, rate limits, temporary DB issues).

## Workflow

1. Set `max_attempts:` to the real retry budget (default 20 is fine).
2. Return `{:error, reason}` when transient — Oban retries with exponential backoff.
3. Override `backoff/1` only when you need custom logic (e.g., rate-limit-aware delays).

See `${CLAUDE_SKILL_DIR}/references/guide.md` for backoff recipes.

## Examples

- `def backoff(%Oban.Job{attempt: a}), do: :math.pow(2, a) |> trunc()` — custom exponential.
- `def backoff(%Oban.Job{unsaved_error: %{reason: :rate_limited}}), do: 300` — slow down on rate limits.

## Common mistakes

- `Process.sleep` + recursive retry inside perform/1 (blocks worker slot).
- `max_attempts: 1` (disables retries entirely).

capability_variants.name: Explicit Period + Safe States
capability_variants.description: Always specify :period. Use the full safe :states list. Align period with the pruner's max_age.
capability_variants.skill_md:
---
name: unique-constraints--explicit-period
description: Unique constraints need explicit :period, full :states list, and pruner alignment. Partial state lists that omit :available are unsafe.
---

# Unique Constraints — Explicit Period

## Quick Start

`unique: [period: 3600, keys: [:id]]` dedupes jobs for one hour. ALWAYS specify period. NEVER use a partial state list that excludes `:available`.

## When to use

- Any worker where duplicate enqueues could cause double work.
- When a user action can fire multiple times (double-click, retry button).

## Workflow

1. Identify the dedupe key (`user_id`, `report_id`, `document_id`).
2. Choose a reasonable period (60s for idempotency-key races, 86400s for per-day uniqueness).
3. Leave `:states` at the default OR use the full safe list.
4. Ensure the pruner's max_age is >= the unique period.

See `${CLAUDE_SKILL_DIR}/references/guide.md` for safe state lists.

## Examples

- `unique: [period: 86_400, keys: [:report_id, :date]]` — once per day per report.

## Common mistakes

- Omitting `:period` (default is 60s, rarely what you want).
- `states: [:scheduled, :executing, :retryable]` — excludes `:available`, crashes queues.
- Unique period > pruner max_age — pruner silently breaks dedup.

capability_variants.name: UTC Cron + Explicit TZ Dependency
capability_variants.description: Oban.Plugins.Cron uses 5-field cron expressions and defaults to UTC. Non-UTC timezones require the tz dependency — document it explicitly.
capability_variants.skill_md:
---
name: cron-scheduling--utc-default
description: Use 5-field cron expressions. Default to UTC. For non-UTC, add tz as a dependency and document it in config. One-minute resolution minimum.
---

# Cron Scheduling — UTC Default

## Quick Start

Oban.Plugins.Cron: 5-field expressions, UTC default, 1-minute minimum resolution, static at boot.

## When to use

- Daily/hourly/weekly jobs on a fixed wall-clock schedule.

## Workflow

1. Write a 5-field cron expression (minute hour day month weekday).
2. Default to UTC.
3. If a timezone is required, add `{:tz, "~> 0.26"}` to mix.exs and call `Calendar.put_time_zone_database(Tz.TimeZoneDatabase)` in Application.start.

See `${CLAUDE_SKILL_DIR}/references/guide.md` for cron format details.

## Examples

- `{"0 3 * * *", MyApp.Workers.NightlyCleanup}` — daily at 3am UTC.
- `{"*/15 * * * *", MyApp.Workers.HealthCheck}` — every 15 minutes.

## Common mistakes

- `"*/30 * * * * *"` — 6 fields (seconds) NOT supported.
- Setting `:timezone` without the tz dependency — crashes on boot.
- Expecting runtime crontab changes — free plugin is static at boot.

capability_variants.name: Full Return Protocol
capability_variants.description: Use :ok, {:error, reason}, {:cancel, reason}, {:snooze, seconds}. Never {:discard, _} (deprecated). Never raise on rate limits.
capability_variants.skill_md:
---
name: return-values--full-protocol
description: The full Oban perform/1 return protocol. Prefer {:cancel, reason} over deprecated {:discard}. Use {:snooze} for rate limits.
---

# Return Values — Full Protocol

## Quick Start

Return `:ok`, `{:ok, _}`, `{:error, reason}`, `{:cancel, reason}`, or `{:snooze, seconds}`. Match each to a failure class.

## When to use

- Every perform/1 clause that can encounter different error shapes.

## Workflow

1. Happy path → `:ok`.
2. Transient (retry please) → `{:error, reason}`.
3. Permanent (stop retrying) → `{:cancel, reason}`.
4. Rate-limited → `{:snooze, seconds}` (NOT raise).

See `${CLAUDE_SKILL_DIR}/references/guide.md` for branch examples.

## Examples

- HTTP 429 → `{:snooze, retry_after}`.
- HTTP 404 (entity gone) → `{:cancel, :not_found}`.
- HTTP 500 → `{:error, {:server, 500}}`.

## Common mistakes

- `{:discard, reason}` — deprecated in 2.17+, use `{:cancel, reason}`.
- `raise` on rate limits — wastes an attempt and fills your error tracker.
- Returning `{:noreply, _}` from GenServer muscle memory.

capability_variants.name: Static Queue Config
capability_variants.description: Queue and max_attempts are static on `use Oban.Worker`. Runtime overrides go through `new/2` options. Priority: 0 = highest, 9 = lowest.
capability_variants.skill_md:
---
name: queues-and-priority--static-config
description: Worker options are compile-time. Never read Application.get_env/2 inside `use Oban.Worker`. Priority 0 is highest, 9 is lowest.
---

# Queues and Priority — Static Config

## Quick Start

`use Oban.Worker` is a macro — its options are frozen at compile time. For runtime-configurable values, pass them to `new/2` instead.

## When to use

- Any worker with configurable queue or max_attempts.

## Workflow

1. Put static defaults on `use Oban.Worker, queue: :default, max_attempts: 3`.
2. In your enqueue helper, read runtime config and pass to `new/2`: `new(args, queue: runtime_queue, max_attempts: runtime_attempts)`.

See `${CLAUDE_SKILL_DIR}/references/guide.md` for priority semantics.

## Examples

- `priority: 0` = critical payments.
- `priority: 9` = background analytics sync.

## Common mistakes

- `use Oban.Worker, queue: Application.get_env(:my_app, :queue)` — frozen at compile time.
- Treating priority 0 as 'none' — it's the HIGHEST.

capability_variants.name: Manual Mode + perform_job
capability_variants.description: Default to :manual mode in test config. Use perform_job/3 for unit tests (it auto-converts atom keys to strings). Use drain_queue/2 for integration.
capability_variants.skill_md:
---
name: testing-workers--manual-mode
description: Use :manual testing mode by default. perform_job/3 for unit tests, drain_queue/2 for integration. Never manually construct %Oban.Job{}.
---

# Testing Workers — Manual Mode

## Quick Start

Set `config :my_app, Oban, testing: :manual` in test config. Use `Oban.Testing` helpers, never build `%Oban.Job{}` by hand.

## When to use

- All worker tests.

## Workflow

1. `use Oban.Testing, repo: MyApp.Repo` in the test module.
2. Unit test: `perform_job(Worker, %{"id" => 1})` — returns the worker's result directly.
3. Enqueue assertion: `assert_enqueued/1` with `worker:` and `args:` keys.
4. Integration: `Oban.drain_queue(queue: :q)` runs all pending jobs.

See `${CLAUDE_SKILL_DIR}/references/guide.md` for sandbox allowances.

## Examples

- `assert :ok = perform_job(Worker, %{"id" => 1})`
- `assert_enqueued worker: Worker, args: %{"id" => 1}`

## Common mistakes

- `:inline` mode + `assert_enqueued` — contradiction, inline jobs bypass DB.
- Hand-building `%Oban.Job{args: _}` — misses the helper's string-key conversion.

capability_variants.name: Attach Many at Application Start
capability_variants.description: Attach telemetry handlers ONCE in Application.start/2 via attach_many/4. Never inside perform/1. Use exact [:oban, :job, :*] event names.
capability_variants.skill_md:
---
name: telemetry-and-observability--attach-many-at-start
description: Attach Oban telemetry handlers in Application.start/2 using :telemetry.attach_many/4 with the exact [:oban, :job, :*] event names.
---

# Telemetry — Attach at Start

## Quick Start

Attach handlers ONCE at boot. Use `:telemetry.attach_many/4` for multiple events. The events are `[:oban, :job, :start]`, `[:oban, :job, :stop]`, `[:oban, :job, :exception]`.

## When to use

- When you need metrics, error reporting, or custom logging for Oban jobs.

## Workflow

1. In Application.start/2, call `:telemetry.attach_many/4` with a static handler id.
2. Define `handle_event/4` with pattern-matching clauses for each event.
3. Keep handler logic minimal (dispatch to metrics/alerting modules).

See `${CLAUDE_SKILL_DIR}/references/guide.md` for error-reporter integrations.

## Examples

- `:telemetry.attach_many("oban-metrics", [[:oban, :job, :start], [:oban, :job, :stop], [:oban, :job, :exception]], &MyApp.ObanHandler.handle_event/4, nil)`

## Common mistakes

- `:telemetry.attach/4` inside `perform/1` — leaks handlers on every job.
- Wrong event name `[:oban, :worker, :exception]` — correct is `[:oban, :job, :exception]`.

capability_variants.name: Ecto.Multi with Oban.insert
capability_variants.description: Use Ecto.Multi + Oban.insert/3 to make data changes and job enqueue atomic. Dependent-step form: `fn %{user: user} -> Worker.new(%{...}) end`.
capability_variants.skill_md:
---
name: transactional-jobs--multi-insert
description: Always use Ecto.Multi + Oban.insert/3 for enqueue alongside data writes. Two-call enqueue (Repo.insert then Oban.insert) is not atomic.
---

# Transactional Jobs — Multi + Oban.insert

## Quick Start

When enqueueing a job alongside data changes, use ONE Ecto.Multi pipeline so both commit or both roll back.

## When to use

- Every context function that inserts data AND enqueues a follow-up job.

## Workflow

1. `Ecto.Multi.new() |> Ecto.Multi.insert(:user, changeset)`
2. `|> Oban.insert(:welcome_email, fn %{user: user} -> Worker.new(%{"user_id" => user.id}) end)`
3. `|> Repo.transaction()`
4. Pattern-match on `{:ok, %{user: user}}` or `{:error, step, value, _}`.

See `${CLAUDE_SKILL_DIR}/references/guide.md` for multi-job flows.

## Examples

- User registration enqueues welcome email atomically with the user insert.

## Common mistakes

- Two-call pattern: `Repo.insert(user); Worker.new(...) |> Oban.insert()` — crashes between steps leave data with no follow-up.
- `Oban.insert_all/1` for bulk unique jobs — basic engine does NOT enforce uniqueness on bulk insert.

capability_variants.name: Reschedule-First for Self-Rescheduling
capability_variants.description: Per-entity recurring jobs reschedule at the START of perform/1 on attempt 1 only. Cron for fixed wall-clock. schedule_in for delayed one-shots.
capability_variants.skill_md:
---
name: recurring-jobs-vs-cron--reschedule-first
description: Choose between cron, schedule_in, and self-rescheduling correctly. Self-rescheduling workers reschedule FIRST on attempt 1 only to avoid retry amplification.
---

# Recurring Jobs vs Cron

## Quick Start

- Cron → fixed wall-clock schedule for ALL entities (e.g., 3am UTC nightly cleanup).
- schedule_in → one-shot delayed job (e.g., 'send welcome 24h after signup').
- Self-rescheduling → per-entity recurring, interval varies per entity.

## When to use

- Cron: daily/hourly jobs that don't depend on per-entity timing.
- schedule_in: single future event tied to an entity.
- Self-reschedule: recurring per-entity jobs (heartbeats, digests).

## Workflow

1. For self-rescheduling: pattern-match `%Oban.Job{attempt: 1}` in a dedicated clause, enqueue the next iteration BEFORE the business logic, then run the business logic.
2. For other attempts (retries), ONLY run the business logic — do not re-enqueue.

See `${CLAUDE_SKILL_DIR}/references/guide.md` for the Sorentwo 'Reliable Scheduling' pattern.

## Examples

- `def perform(%Oban.Job{attempt: 1, args: args}) do enqueue_next(args); run_logic(args); end`

## Common mistakes

- Rescheduling at the END of perform/1 — retries amplify the reschedule exponentially.
- Using cron for per-entity intervals that depend on user data.
- Sub-minute cron expressions (`*/30 * * * * *`) — not supported.
